Strip_Summary: Stickman son in bed whispering numbers into pillow at night. Dim warm bedroom light.
Bold text on dark: 'He thinks it is a game. You don't correct him. Because he's right.'
Stickman at startup desk designing app buttons. Tel Aviv office with delivery map on screen.
Driver checking shelter map on phone. Someone else designed that part. Stickman does buttons.
Stickman on train platform arguing about basketball scores. Morning commute, HaShalom station.
Bringing rugelach home from Dizengoff Friday. Stepping on LEGO at 2 AM barefoot. Wife asleep.
Bold text on dark: 'You are ordinary. That is the point.'
Bold text on dark red: 'It gives you ninety seconds.'
First-person view of apartment hallway stretching ahead. Red siren glow through windows.
Wide Tel Aviv view: bustling Rothschild Boulevard on Friday afternoon. Crowds, cafes, palm trees.
Students drinking iced coffee on Ibn Gabirol. Couples on promenade at sunset, Mediterranean behind.
Mediterranean coastline: sea indifferent to borders. Waves rolling, golden evening light on water.
Stickman on promenade wiping salt off phone screen with shirt. Casual seaside moment, warm breeze.
Stickman on train with angry coworker gesturing about Maccabi loss. HaShalom platform, morning rush.
Arguing about roster on platform while train late. Corner cafe barista drawing smiley on cup.
Same smiley face every time on cup. Rugelach from Dizengoff every Friday. Walking home with bag.
Wife says too many rugelach. Stickman eating three on sidewalk walk home anyway. Warm evening.
Dark apartment: LEGO on floor. Stickman stepping on piece, wincing. Saying something regrettable.
Tel Aviv skyline: conflict always there, like traffic or heat. Background noise nobody notices.
Stickman stops noticing after a while. Or thinks he does. City continuing, tension invisible.
Iron Dome working: white streaks in sky. Sirens come and go. City continues below streets.
Stickman son looking up at sky casually during siren. Born into this. Sirens are weather to him.
Stickman on apartment balcony at golden hour. Hand on railing, gazing at skyline. Cool blue wash.
Bold text on warm amber: 'You will not understand what this city was until it stops being this.'
Phone screen cascading Red Alert notifications: Monday 2, Tuesday 5, Wednesday 11. Escalating count.
Stickman son pointing at buzzing phone: Daddy's phone singing. Father auto-swiping alert dismissal.
Father staring at phone. Not remembering when swiping became automatic. Distant expression, kitchen.
Empty Dizengoff street: shuttered restaurants, closed school gate, silent delivery app on phone.
Phone screen showing app: buttons nobody presses. Designed interface sitting idle. Empty delivery grid.
Bold text on dark: 'The frequency is the weapon. Not the rockets.'
Dark apartment 4:17 AM: siren wailing. Stickman body up before brain. Bare feet on cold floor.
Cold hallway: bare feet on tiles. Not feeling them until hallway. Red glow bleeding through walls.
Sleeping son heavy and warm on shoulder. Face against neck. Shampoo smell. Cool blue wash fading.
Wife with go-bag following silently through dark hallway. Neither speaks. Moving toward mamad door.
Stickman pulling stuck mamad door: pull, lift, yank. Metal handle, desperate sequential motion.
Hand slipping on cold metal handle. Sting ignored. Half a second cannot be spent looking down.
Inside sealed mamad: concrete walls. Eight square meters. Door closed. Family enclosed together.
Mamad interior details: LEGO set on floor, water bottles lined, flashlight ready. Second home prepared.
Stickman in mamad, heart in throat. Hearing own heartbeat. Son beside him. Desaturated grey vignette.
Three cracks overhead: Iron Dome intercepts. Pressure drops in ears. Pop of survival above.
Sulfur seeping through window seal. Smell hitting nostrils before all-clear. Chemical residue haze.
Stickman safe but shaking in mamad. Will shake for another hour. Son asleep on mamad floor beside.
Bold text on dark: 'Ninety seconds felt like forever.'
Balcony at night: son watching Iron Dome interceptions. White streaks crossing sky like angry fireflies.
Son says cool. Father says nothing. Two reactions to same sky. Balcony railing between them.
Father gripping railing: two hundred rockets, ninety percent, twenty impacts somewhere in paese.
Interception flash above: ears pop, sulfur in nostrils. Son clapping. Father's chest tightening.
Stickman watching survival calculated overhead in real time. Eyes upward, rigid body. Cool blue wash.
World says relax about Iron Dome. Stickman cannot relax. Feeling ninety percent, not one hundred.
Bold text on dark: 'Ninety percent is not safe. Math that waits for the wrong night.'
Day seven: body runs countdown without thinking. Siren, feet, son on shoulder. Mechanical sequence.
Hallway, door, pull lift yank. Seven seconds. Two seconds faster than first night. Practiced route.
Feet knowing path in dark. Hands finding handle without looking. No more shaking. Muscle memory.
Stickman in mamad doorway. Should feel proud of efficiency. Does not. Cold grey vignette edges.
Son sleeping through pickup. Not stirring when lifted. Not waking when carried. Body adapted to routine.
Father holding son one second longer than needed. Something learned. Something haunting. Cool blue wash.
Father holding sleeping son in mamad longer than necessary. Ninety seconds used to mean something.
Bold text on dark: 'Ninety seconds used to feel like something. You don't remember when that changed.'
Neighbor at apartment door with military bag. Miluim. Reserve duty called. Preparing to depart.
Neighbor kissing daughter at door. Then driving north. Quick goodbye, long uncertainty ahead.
Neighbor's wife standing frozen in hallway holding daughter. Jaw set, hands shaking. Door closed behind.
Stickman bringing dinner to neighbor's door that night. No words. Standing with food container, doorway.
Mamad during siren: two children playing LEGO. Daughter builds house. Son builds dome over it.
Two weeks later: dark phone screen, messages unanswered. No questions asked. Silence understood.
Stickman on couch watching news at low volume. Son nearby. Casualties without names scrolling screen.
Father counting names not his neighbor's. Hope by subtraction. By subtraction. Desaturated grey vignette.
Phone screen: sister in New York on video call, crying. Upset and afraid. Distant connection.
Through sister's phone: colleagues arguing about war. She doesn't know what to say when asked to condemn.
Stickman in mamad at 2 AM holding phone. Son asleep on chest. Twenty-two kilos. Warm breath on neck.
Close shot: son breathing on father's neck. Shampoo smell. Something burning outside seen through slit.
Orange glow through mamad window slit. Something burning. Phone cutting out. Don't know either.
World has opinions. Stickman has mamad and ninety seconds and boy. Not a headline. Cool blue wash.
Father in mamad: 22 kilos on chest, phone dead, holding son tighter. Signal lost. Concrete room.
Bold text on dark: 'You're not a headline. You're not a position.'
Bold text on dark: 'The concrete is the only thing between his sleep and the sky.'
Mamad moment: one gets through. Hearing difference instantly. Impact not interception. Different sound.
Interception versus impact: crack above versus vibration through skeleton. Sound in bones, different register.
Floor shifting beneath family. Not shaking - shifting. Building exhaled. Something structural. Dust falling.
Ears ringing, car alarm screaming outside continuously. Not stopping. Post-impact disorientation, dark room.
Bold text on dark: 'Eight hundred meters from your son's school.'
Stickman driving past crater near supermarket. School already closed, keeps forgetting that. Detour signs.
Twisted playground fence. Swing set on side. Backpack hanging on fence. Nobody's. Everyone's. Grey vignette.
Son looking through car window at damaged playground. Silent. No words. Just seeing the destruction.
That night: son walking into mamad without being told. Lying down on blanket himself. Self-directed.
Son not counting to ninety. Father waiting for count to begin. It does not start. Cold grey vignette.
Bold text on dark: 'That is worse.'
TV showing rubble. Buildings that look familiar. Destruction matching home architecture. News footage.
TV footage: children pulled from concrete. Resembling son. Same age, same size. Screen light on face.
Living room: stickman relieved it isn't his building. Not looking at wife. Self-aware guilty relief.
Wife says turn it off. TV stays on. Tension between needing to know and needing to stop watching.
Loose cable behind TV noticed. Looking at it one second. Looking away. Small fixation on mundane detail.
TV off. Walking to bed in dark hallway. Day ending with nothing resolved. Shadow filling corridor.
Elevator: three stickmen standing in silence. Half-second eye contact. Everyone carrying invisible weight.
Stickman in communal silence. Not asking. Not talking. Weight of unspoken dread shared. Grey vignette.
Dizengoff cafe reopened after ten days. Same barista, same apron. Something shifted behind her eyes.
Same coffee ordered. Same chair chosen. Trying to recover normal at familiar table. Cafe warm light.
Hand wrapping around warm cup. Only warmth that feels normal. Steam rising from coffee. Brief peace.
Siren at cafe: everyone stands with cups. Walking to shelter. Not running. Walking. Casual evacuation.
After all-clear: walking back. Coffee still warm. Back at table. Resuming as if nothing happened.
Stickman drinking coffee deliberately. If he stops pretending nothing happened he will scream inside.
Son asking about booms: good ones or bad ones? Six years old. Correct categorization. Asking calmly.
Two kinds of booms confirmed. Son learned the difference in two weeks. Father answering: the good ones.
Son nodding, going back to LEGO. Building something new. Small hands constructing carefully in mamad.
Son building dome out of LEGO. Father watching, asking what it is. Small careful construction taking shape.
Tiny LEGO figure placed inside dome. 'That is you Daddy.' Figure smiling. Safe under plastic dome.
Bold text on dark: 'The dome is made of plastic.'
Stickman alone in bathroom. Door closed. Forehead against mirror. Hands on sink edge. Grey tint.
Hands gripping sink edge. Porcelain cold under fingers. Body processing what mind cannot. Grey vignette.
Something catching in chest - not pain - a skip. Then gone. Breathing deeply. Warm amber glow edges.
Hallway at night: stepping on LEGO piece. Same action as before but everything feels different now.
Post-ceasefire: expecting change. Nothing changing. Same apartment, same street, same sky overhead.
Fixed playground with new fence and swings. Son refuses to go near it. Walking past, avoiding it.
Cafe open. Different barista - original moved to Berlin. Nobody told him. Just new face behind counter.
Coffee without smiley face. Same taste. Stickman drinking it anyway. Quiet resignation at familiar spot.
Neighbor back from miluim. Doesn't talk about where he was. Smokes on balcony at 3 AM. Changed posture.
Night: two adjacent balconies. Neighbor's cigarette glow visible. Stickman watching. Neither waves.
Two stickmen on separate balconies, city lights below. Both awake at 3 AM. Both knowing why. Silent.
Bedroom: wife asleep with hand on go-bag. Tried moving it once. She moved it back without words.
Mamad shelf: LEGO dome with tiny figure inside. Still smiling. Untouched monument on concrete shelf.
Mirror of HOOK shot: son in bed counting to ninety. Father in doorway. Same pose, heavier expression.
Bold text on dark: 'That is how you know something broke.'
Living room: car door slams outside. Stickman flinching. Chest tightening instantly. Phantom trigger.
Feet moving toward hallway before brain says stop. Automatic response against conscious will. Muscle memory.
Stickman frozen in dark hallway. Light off, door closed. Everything blurs. Cannot remember if ceasefire real or dream. Grey tint.
Walking back. Not mentioning it. Body carrying what mind left behind. Warm amber at edges.
Bold text on black: 'The sirens stopped.' (beat) 'Your body did not.'
Stickman at apartment window. City below, dome held. Hand touching glass pane. Morning light coming.
Bold text on dark: 'It is not.'
